1: "If there is a dispute between men and they come into court and the judges decide between them, acquitting the innocent (tzadiq) and condemning the guilty (rasha)" — the judicial process premise: the court (mishpat) functions by distinguishing the righteous from the wicked. Tzaddiq and rasha are not merely legal categories (innocent/guilty) but covenant categories: the righteous one lives in covenant faithfulness; the wicked one violates it. The court is the institution that aligns legal outcomes with covenant reality. Romans 3:4: "that you may be justified in your words and prevail when you are judged" — the Psalmic pattern of YHWH as the righteous judge is the ground of the human judicial system.
2: "Then if the guilty man deserves to be beaten, the judge shall cause him to lie down and be beaten in his presence with a number of stripes in proportion to his offense" — the corporal punishment law: the flogging (makah) must occur in the judge's presence (preventing private revenge or excessive punishment), must be proportional to the offense, and is subject to the limit that follows. The judge's presence at the punishment is a procedural protection: the punishment is a judicial act, not a social one. 2 Corinthians 11:24: Paul reports receiving "forty lashes less one" five times from Jewish authorities — showing that Deuteronomy 25:2-3's legal punishment was actively administered in the Second Temple period.
3: "Forty stripes may be given him, but not more, lest, if one should go on to beat him with more stripes than these, your brother be degraded in your sight" — the forty-stripe maximum: the limit protects human dignity. venikleh achikha le'einecha (lest your brother be degraded before your eyes): the convict remains a brother even during punishment — the covenant brotherhood is not dissolved by wrongdoing. The maximum is not an optimal punishment but a dignity-floor that legal punishment cannot breach. Jewish practice implemented it as "forty less one" (39) to prevent accidental breach of the limit (hence Paul's "forty lashes less one").
4: "You shall not muzzle an ox when it is treading out the grain" — the ox-muzzle prohibition: lo tachsom shor bedisho. The ox working to thresh grain is allowed to eat from the grain it processes. 1 Corinthians 9:9: "For it is written in the Law of Moses, 'You shall not muzzle an ox when it treads out the grain.' Is it for oxen that God is concerned?" 1 Timothy 5:18: "For the Scripture says, 'You shall not muzzle an ox when it treads out the grain,' and, 'The laborer deserves his wages.'" Paul's application: DIRECT citation, with explicit hermeneutical move — if even working animals deserve to eat from their labor, how much more do human ministers deserve material support from those they serve. The principle (labor generates right to support) transcends the specific application (ox in grain) and applies to the more-than-ox minister of the gospel.
5: "If brothers dwell together, and one of them dies and has no son, the wife of the dead man shall not be married outside the family to a stranger. Her husband's brother shall go in to her and take her as his wife and perform the duty of a husband's brother to her" — the levirate marriage law (yibbum, from yavam, H2992, husband's brother): the brother-in-law takes the dead brother's widow as wife to produce offspring that will carry the dead brother's name and inheritance. Levirate is from Latin levir (husband's brother). Matthew 22:24-28: the Sadducees pose the levirate-marriage scenario (a woman successively married to seven brothers) as a reductio ad absurdum against resurrection. Jesus's response ("in the resurrection they neither marry nor are given in marriage, but are like angels in heaven") does not abandon levirate law but reframes the eschatological situation: resurrection existence transcends the social-economic-kinship framework within which levirate marriage was necessary.
6: "And the firstborn son whom she bears shall succeed to the name of his dead brother, that his name may not be blotted out from Israel" — the levirate purpose: preserving the dead brother's name (shem) and inheritance-line within Israel. The fear of name-obliteration reflects the ancient understanding of death-in-memory as the continuation of existence: to have no name remembered is to be truly dead. The levirate is therefore a covenantal act of resurrection-by-memory: the dead man's line continues, his name lives. This concern for name-preservation echoes Isaiah 56:5's promise to eunuchs: "I will give them an everlasting name that shall not be cut off" — the new covenant provides the ultimate levirate, a name preserved forever in YHWH's own memory.
7: "And if the man does not wish to take his brother's wife, then his brother's wife shall go up to the gate to the elders and say, 'My husband's brother refuses to perpetuate his brother's name in Israel; he will not perform the duty of a husband's brother to me'" — the judicial process for levirate refusal: the widow brings the case to the elders. The law does not force the brother but creates a public process for declining the duty — which comes with public cost (the sandal-removal ceremony and the shaming of v9-10). The widow has legal agency to initiate the process, not merely to await the brother's decision.
8: "Then the elders of his city shall call him and speak to him, and if he persists, saying, 'I do not wish to take her'" — the elders' mediation: the community attempts persuasion before the public ceremony. The elders' role is pastoral before it is judicial: speaking to the reluctant brother, not immediately imposing the shame-ceremony. The law provides a pathway for declining that, while shameful, is nonetheless legal — the covenant does not impose absolute compulsion but does impose significant social cost for declining a covenant duty.
9: "Then his brother's wife shall go up to him in the presence of the elders and pull his sandal off his foot and spit in his face. And she shall answer and say, 'So shall it be done to the man who does not build up his brother's house'" — the chalitzah ceremony (sandal-removal): the public shaming of the brother who refuses levirate duty. The sandal removal reverses the sandal-ceremony of property transfer (Ruth 4:7-8: removing a sandal was the traditional way to confirm a transfer). The reversed ceremony (she removes his sandal) publicly withdraws him from his covenant responsibility. John 1:27: John the Baptist's statement that he is "not worthy to untie the sandal" of the one coming after him invokes this Deuteronomic sandal-language — the sandal is the symbol of standing, authority, and right of succession. John disclaims his own standing relative to Christ's.
10: "And the name of his family shall be called in Israel, 'The house of him who had his sandal pulled off'" — the permanent family-name consequence: the shame attaches not just to the individual but to his family line. The naming is the covenantal memory-consequence of failing covenant duty. The law creates communal accountability through naming: families are remembered by their covenant faithfulness or failure. The positive counterpart is Boaz's memorial: "to perpetuate the name of the dead in his inheritance" (Ruth 4:10) — Boaz takes the kinsman-redeemer role that the nearer kinsman declined (Ruth 4:6), and Boaz's name is preserved in the Messiah's genealogy (Matthew 1:5).
11: "When men fight with one another and the wife of the one draws near to rescue her husband from the hand of him who is beating him and puts out her hand and seizes him by his private parts" — the fight-intervention scenario: a woman intervenes in a fight to protect her husband by grabbing the opponent's genitals. The legal scenario sets up the unusual penalty in v12. The extreme intervention represents a combination of intent (protecting her husband) and means (a method that requires specific legal address because of the social and physical implications).
12: "Then you shall cut off her hand. Your eye shall have no pity" — the hand-amputation penalty: the only explicit amputation penalty in the Torah. The combination of the body-part targeted (reproductive) and the penalty (hand) creates a proportional response in kind. The no-pity formula (lo tachos einkha) appears elsewhere for extreme violations that require full judicial consequence without sentiment. The law is rarely discussed and its application in Israel's legal history is uncertain — it may function as a maximum-deterrent rather than a regularly applied penalty.
13: "You shall not have in your bag two kinds of weights, a large and a small" — the honest-weights legislation (vv13-16): the false-weight prohibition. Even vaeven (weight and weight) — carrying two sets of weights (larger for buying, smaller for selling) is covenant fraud. Proverbs 11:1: "A false balance is an abomination to the LORD, but a just weight is his delight." Amos 8:5: "making the ephah small and the shekel great and dealing deceitfully with false balances" — the prophetic indictment uses the Deuteronomic standard as the baseline for commercial ethics. The marketplace is a covenant space: the same integrity required in the courts is required in commerce.
14: "You shall not have in your house two kinds of measures, a large and a small" — the honest-measures extension: not just weights (for precious metals/grain by mass) but measures (for volume). The double prohibitions (weights and measures) cover all quantitative commercial transactions. The covenant's integrity-demand extends into every commercial interaction, not merely into formal legal proceedings.
15: "A full and fair weight you shall have, a full and fair measure you shall have, that your days may be long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you" — the positive formulation with life-extension promise: even shlemah vatzedek yihye lach (a full/whole and righteous weight shall you have). Shlem (whole, complete) and tzedek (righteous/just) are combined: commercial integrity is covenantal righteousness. The same life-extension promise attached to honoring parents (Deuteronomy 5:16) and bird-nest mercy (Deuteronomy 22:7) is here attached to honest commerce: the covenant weaves together family, ecological, and commercial ethics under the same blessing-logic.
16: "For all who do such things, all who act dishonestly, are an abomination to the LORD your God" — toevat YHWH eloheicha: the commercial fraudster commits the same category of covenant violation as the idolater. The toevah term marks commercial dishonesty as a first-order covenant issue, not a minor infraction. The God who is himself emet (truth/faithfulness) is offended by the false weight that contradicts truth in commercial exchange. Proverbs 16:11: "A just balance and scales are the LORD's; all the weights in the bag are his work." The weights are under YHWH's direct sovereignty.
17: "Remember what Amalek did to you on the way as you came out of Egypt" — the Amalek-memory command (vv17-19): the counter-command to the general Egyptian non-abhorrence (Deuteronomy 23:7). Amalek attacked Israel at Rephidim unprovoked and specifically targeted the weak and weary at the rear (v18). The attack on the vulnerable is the defining feature that warrants permanent covenantal hostility. Numbers 24:20: Balaam's oracle — "Amalek was the first among the nations, but its end is utter destruction." 1 Samuel 15: Saul's incomplete execution of the Amalek-command becomes the occasion for his rejection as king. The Amalek-command represents the theological problem of divine command for corporate destruction.
18: "How he attacked you on the way when you were faint and weary, and cut off your tail, those who were lagging behind you, and he did not fear God" — the specific indictment: Amalek attacked the weak stragglers — those least able to defend themselves. Velo yare Elohim (and he did not fear God): the absence of the fear of God is the theological diagnosis of Amalek's attack. Those who do not fear God attack the vulnerable; those who fear God protect them. The Deuteronomic ethics of vulnerable-protection (Deuteronomy 24:17-21) and the Amalek command share the same logic: Amalek is the anti-type of Deuteronomy's covenant ethics.
19: "Therefore when the LORD your God has given you rest from all your enemies around you, in the land that the LORD your God is giving you for an inheritance to possess, you shall blot out the memory of Amalek from under heaven; you shall not forget" — the Amalek-obliteration command: timcheh et-zecher Amalek (blot out the memory of Amalek). The irony of the command: "blot out the memory" + "you shall not forget" creates a permanent memorial of the obligation to destroy the memory. The command creates the very memory it seeks to eradicate. 1 Samuel 15's partial-execution by Saul and the subsequent Agag-execution by Samuel represent the covenant's insistence that divine commands be fully executed, not partially — a theme Paul uses in Galatians 3:10 ("Cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the Book of the Law, to do them").
